revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した土のことをいう。覆土には、その遺構に...'
revision.text:
'''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した土のことをいう。覆土には、その遺構に関する情報が含まれている場合もあれば、ただ単に流れ込んだ土である場合もあり、遺構の性格や廃絶過程を知る情報が含まれている。
一般的に、覆土は、その遺構の廃絶がゆっくり行われたか、またどのように行われたのか、[[地下式坑]]や[[窯]]跡などは天井が崩落したのかということを発掘調査によって知ることができる。通常は、覆土にセクションベルト（土層ベルト）というあぜを残して調査を行い、その土層観察を通して遺構の埋没過程、廃絶過程を推定する。
覆土には遺構に関する特殊な情報が含まれている場合とは、たとえば火災で焼け落ちたり廃棄された[[竪穴式住居|竪穴住居跡]]には、その住居に使われた木材が火で焼け落ちて覆土に含まれている場合があり、良好な場合は屋根に土や萱が使われていたとか、焼け落ちた順番まで知ることができる。その場合の覆土中の木材によって、どのような樹種の木材がどのような上屋構造で使われたか、また運がよければ[[年輪年代学|年輪年代]]と[[放射性炭素年代測定]]に使えるサンプルとなる。また[[珪藻]]や[[花粉]]などの微化石で当時の環境までわかる場合もある。
[[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴住居跡が貝層で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は住居の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは住居が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]氏によって唱えられた。
[[category:考古学|ふくと]]

[[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴住居跡が貝層で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は住居の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは住居が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]氏によって唱えられた。

'''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した土のことをいう。覆土には、その遺構に関する情報が含まれている場合もあれば、ただ単に流れ込んだ土である場合もあり、遺構の性格や廃絶過程を知る情報が含まれている。 一般的に、覆土は、その遺構の廃絶がゆっくり行われたか、またどのように行われたのか、[[地下式坑]]や[[窯]]跡などは天井が崩落したのかということを[[発掘調査]]によって知ることができる。通常は、覆土にセクションベルト（土層ベルト）というあぜを残して調査を行い、その[[実測図#遺構の実測図|土層観察]]を通して遺構の埋没過程、廃絶過程を推定するこのような覆土の堆積状況を実測して表現した断面図をセクション図と呼ぶ。。 覆土には遺構に関する特殊な情報が含まれている場合とは、たとえば火災で焼け落ちたり廃棄された[[竪穴式住居|竪穴住居跡]]には、その住居に使われた木材が火で焼け落ちて覆土に含まれている場合があり、良好な場合は屋根に土や萱が使われていたとか、焼け落ちた順番まで知ることができる。その場合の覆土中の木材によって、どのような樹種の木材がどのような上屋構造で使われたか、また運がよければ[[年輪年代学|年輪年代]]と[[放射性炭素年代測定]]に使えるサンプルとなる。また[[珪藻]]や[[花粉]]などの微化石で当時の環境までわかる場合もある。 [[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴住居跡が貝層で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は住居の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは住居が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]氏によって唱えられた。 ==脚注==

'''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した土のことをいう。覆土には、その遺構に関する情報が含まれている場合もあれば、ただ単に流れ込んだ土である場合もあり、遺構の性格や廃絶過程を知る情報が含まれている。 一般的に、覆土は、その遺構の廃絶がゆっくり行われたか、またどのように行われたのか、[[地下式坑]]や[[窯]]跡などは天井が崩落したのかということを[[発掘調査]]によって知ることができる。通常は、覆土にセクションベルト（土層ベルト）というあぜを残して調査を行い、その[[実測図#遺構の実測図|土層観察]]を通して遺構の埋没過程、廃絶過程を推定するこのような覆土の堆積状況を実測して表現した断面図をセクション図と呼ぶ。。 覆土には遺構に関する特殊な情報が含まれている場合とは、たとえば火災で焼け落ちたり廃棄された[[竪穴式住居|竪穴住居跡]]には、その住居に使われた木材が火で焼け落ちて覆土に含まれている場合があり、良好な場合は屋根に土や萱が使われていたとか、焼け落ちた順番まで知ることができる。その場合の覆土中の木材によって、どのような樹種の木材がどのような上屋構造で使われたか、また運がよければ[[年輪年代学|年輪年代]]と[[放射性炭素年代測定]]に使えるサンプルとなる。また[[珪藻]]や[[花粉]]などの微化石で当時の環境までわかる場合もある。 [[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴住居跡が貝層で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は住居の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは住居が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]によって唱えられた。 ==脚注==

'''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した土のことをいう。覆土には、その遺構に関する情報が含まれている場合もあれば、ただ単に流れ込んだ土である場合もあり、遺構の性格や廃絶過程を知る情報が含まれている。 一般的に、覆土は、その遺構の廃絶がゆっくり行われたか、またどのように行われたのか、[[地下式坑]]や[[窯]]跡などは天井が崩落したのかということを[[発掘調査]]によって知ることができる。通常は、覆土にセクションベルト（土層ベルト）というあぜを残して調査を行い、その[[実測図 (考古学)|土層観察]]を通して遺構の埋没過程、廃絶過程を推定するこのような覆土の堆積状況を実測して表現した断面図をセクション図と呼ぶ。。 覆土には遺構に関する特殊な情報が含まれている場合とは、たとえば火災で焼け落ちたり廃棄された[[竪穴式住居|竪穴住居跡]]には、その住居に使われた木材が火で焼け落ちて覆土に含まれている場合があり、良好な場合は屋根に土や萱が使われていたとか、焼け落ちた順番まで知ることができる。その場合の覆土中の木材によって、どのような樹種の木材がどのような上屋構造で使われたか、また運がよければ[[年輪年代学|年輪年代]]と[[放射性炭素年代測定]]に使えるサンプルとなる。また[[珪藻]]や[[花粉]]などの微化石で当時の環境までわかる場合もある。 [[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴住居跡が貝層で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は住居の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは住居が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]によって唱えられた。 ==脚注==
{{考古学}} [[Category:考古学|ふくと]]

'''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した土のことをいう。「[[埋土]]（うめど・うめつち・まいど）」とも言う[[考古学]]や[[発掘調査]]の現場では、「覆土」と「埋土」は遺構内の堆積土を表す言葉としてほぼ同義的に使われており、例えば下記に挙げた参考文献『発掘調査のてびき』では、「埋土（覆土）」として記述している（同書94頁）。。 ==概要== 覆土には、その遺構に関する情報が含まれている場合もあれば、ただ単に流れ込んだ土である場合もあり、遺構の性格や廃絶過程を知る情報が含まれている。一般的に、覆土は、その遺構の廃絶がゆっくり行われたか、またどのように行われたのか、[[地下式坑]]や[[窯]]跡などは天井が崩落したのかということを[[発掘調査]]によって知ることができる。通常は、覆土にセクションベルト（土層ベルト）というあぜを残して調査を行い、その[[実測図 (考古学)|土層観察]]を通して遺構の埋没過程、廃絶過程を推定する{{Sfn|文化庁|2010|pp=93-116}}このような覆土の堆積状況を実測して表現した断面図をセクション図と呼ぶ。。 覆土には遺構に関する特殊な情報が含まれている場合とは、たとえば火災で焼け落ちたり廃棄された[[竪穴式住居|竪穴住居跡]]には、その住居に使われた木材が火で焼け落ちて覆土に含まれている場合があり、良好な場合は屋根に土や萱が使われていたとか、焼け落ちた順番まで知ることができる。その場合の覆土中の木材によって、どのような樹種の木材がどのような上屋構造で使われたか、また運がよければ[[年輪年代学|年輪年代]]と[[放射性炭素年代測定]]に使えるサンプルとなる。また[[珪藻]]や[[花粉]]などの微化石で当時の環境までわかる場合もある。 [[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴住居跡が貝層で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は住居の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは住居が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]によって唱えられた。 ==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}}

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Otheruses|[[Archaeology]] terminology for overburden|Other uses|覆土}} '''Fukudo''' (覆土, Overburden) refers to the [[soil]] (or [[strata]]) deposited within [[features]] in the context of [[archaeology]]. It is also called {{ill|Emido|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど)<ref>In [[archaeology]] and [[excavation]] contexts, "overburden" and "buried soil" are often used synonymously to refer to the soil accumulated within features. For example, in the..."

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]]. It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]]. It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Kk]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]]. It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:Pages of uncertain originality]]

For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]]

When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages to be translated]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:pages to be translated]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}}

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]]. It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}}

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}}

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]]. It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:Pages of u]][[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} [[Category:New]]

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} [[Category:New]]

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:New]]

Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]][[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土}} {{Archaeology}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}}

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|12=draft}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構|12=draft}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=draft}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{Archaeology}}

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|12=draft}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構|12=draft}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=draft}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=draft}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯|12=draft}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査|12=draft}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=draft}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学|12=draft}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定|12=draft}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻|12=draft}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉|12=draft}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=draft}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=draft}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|12=draft}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構|12=draft}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=draft}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{Archaeology}}

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|12=draft}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=draft}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構|12=draft}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=draft}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|12=draft}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構|1=Feature_(archaeology)|lt=Archaeological Feature|12=draft}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=draft}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Overburden (archaeology)}} {{Archaeology}}

13 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:覆土 (考古学) up to rev 95512445
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|[[考古学]]における覆土|その他の用法|覆土}} '''覆土（ふくど）'''とは、主に[[考古学]]の用語で、[[遺構]]の内部に[[堆積]]した[[土壌|土]]（[[土層 (考古学)|土層]]）のことをいう。「[[埋土]]（うめど・うめつち・まいど）」とも言う[[考古学]]や[[発掘調査]]の現場では、「覆土」と「埋土」は遺構内の堆積土を表す言葉としてほぼ同義的に使われており、例えば下記に挙げた参考文献『発掘調査のてびき』では、「埋土（覆土）」として記述している（同書94頁）。。 ==概要== 覆土には、その遺構に関する情報が含まれている場合もあれば、ただ単に流れ込んだ土である場合もあり、遺構の性格や廃絶過程を知る情報が含まれている。一般的に、覆土は、その遺構の廃絶がゆっくり行われたか、またどのように行われたのか、[[地下式坑]]や[[窯]]跡などは天井が崩落したのかということを[[発掘調査]]によって知ることができる。通常は、覆土にセクションベルト（土層ベルト）というあぜを残して調査を行い、その[[実測図 (考古学)|土層観察]]を通して遺構の埋没過程、廃絶過程を推定する{{Sfn|文化庁|2010|pp=93-116}}このような覆土の堆積状況を実測して表現した断面図をセクション図と呼ぶ。。 覆土には遺構に関する特殊な情報が含まれている場合とは、たとえば火災で焼け落ちたり廃棄された[[竪穴建物]]跡には、その建物に使われた木材が火で焼け落ちて覆土に含まれている場合があり、良好な場合は屋根に土や萱が使われていたとか、焼け落ちた順番まで知ることができる。その場合の覆土中の木材によって、どのような樹種の木材がどのような上屋構造で使われたか、また運がよければ[[年輪年代学|年輪年代]]と[[放射性炭素年代測定]]に使えるサンプルとなる。また[[珪藻]]や[[花粉]]などの微化石で当時の環境までわかる場合もある。 [[埼玉県]][[和光市]]に所在する[[吹上貝塚]]では、竪穴建物跡が[[貝層]]で埋まっていてその下から完形に近い土器が出土し、その下は建物の床面まで遺物のない層が覆土として観察された。それは建物が構築され、一定期間使用された後、廃絶されて埋まっていく過程で土器の廃棄される場所となり、さらには貝の廃棄される場所になるという人間の行動のサイクルの一定のパターンが見られるという「吹上パターン」もこの覆土の観察から[[小林達雄]]によって唱えられた。 ==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=文化庁|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=第Ⅳ章：土層の認識と表土・包含層の発掘|title=発掘調査のてびき-集落遺跡発掘編-|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[同成社]]|isbn=9784886215253}} ==関連項目== *[[切り合い関係]] *[[遺構]] *[[埋土]] {{考古学}} [[Category:考古学|ふくと]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/覆土|qq=}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|qq=}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構|1=Feature_(archaeology)|lt=Archaeological Feature|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature (archaeology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature_(archaeology)|qq=}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|qq=}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kiln|qq=}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/archaeological excavation|qq=}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|qq=}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tree-ring dating|qq=}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/radiocarbon dating|qq=}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diatoms|qq=}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pollen|qq=}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|qq=}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wako City|qq=}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構|1=Feature_(archaeology)|lt=Archaeological Feature|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature (archaeology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature_(archaeology)|qq=}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/覆土|qq=}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|qq=}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|lt=feature|ja|遺構|1=Feature_(archaeology)|lt=Archaeological Feature|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature (archaeology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature_(archaeology)|qq=}} in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|qq=}} or {{ill|kiln|lt=kiln ruins|ja|窯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kiln|qq=}} collapsed. Through {{ill|archaeological excavation|ja|発掘調査|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/archaeological excavation|qq=}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|qq=}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|tree-ring dating|ja|年輪年代学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tree-ring dating|qq=}} and {{ill|radiocarbon dating|ja|放射性炭素年代測定|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/radiocarbon dating|qq=}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|diatoms|ja|珪藻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diatoms|qq=}} and {{ill|pollen|ja|花粉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pollen|qq=}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|qq=}} in {{ill|Wako City|ja|和光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wako City|qq=}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|ja|遺構|1=Feature_(archaeology)|lt=Archaeological Feature|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature (archaeology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Feature_(archaeology)|qq=}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Stratigraphy]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/覆土|qq=}} in [[archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|qq=}}) that accumulate within a [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] in [[archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|qq=}} or [[:en:Kiln|kiln ruins]] collapsed. Through [[:en:Archaeological excavation|archaeological excavation]], researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. The material may also serve as samples for [[:en:Dendrochronology|tree-ring dating]] and [[:en:Radiocarbon dating|radiocarbon dating]]. Furthermore, microfossils such as [[:en:Diatom|diatoms]] and [[:en:Pollen|pollen]] may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|qq=}} in [[:en:Wakō, Saitama|Wako City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==

This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/覆土|qq=}} in [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|qq=}}) that accumulate within a [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] in [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or [[:en:Kiln|kiln ruins]] collapsed. Through [[:en:Archaeological excavation|archaeological excavation]], researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for [[:en:Dendrochronology|tree-ring dating]] and [[:en:Radiocarbon dating|radiocarbon dating]]. Furthermore, microfossils such as [[:en:Diatom|diatoms]] and [[:en:Pollen|pollen]] may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Wakō, Saitama|Wako City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Overburden (archaeology)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]]

revision.text:
[[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11630076]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|{{ill|覆土|ja|覆土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/覆土|qq=|1=覆土|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630075}} in [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]]|other uses|覆土}} '''Overburden Fukudo''' (覆土) refers to layers of [[:en:Soil|Soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|qq=|1=stratigraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] in [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or [[:en:Kiln|kiln ruins]] collapsed. Through [[:en:Archaeological excavation|archaeological excavation]], researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for [[:en:Dendrochronology|tree-ring dating]] and [[:en:Radiocarbon dating|radiocarbon dating]]. Furthermore, microfossils such as [[:en:Diatom|diatoms]] and [[:en:Pollen|pollen]] may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Wakō, Saitama|Wako City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}}

[[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11630076]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|'''Overburden'''|覆土|Fukudo}} refers to layers of [[:en:Soil|Soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|qq=|1=stratigraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] in [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or [[:en:Kiln|kiln ruins]] collapsed.
