When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for [[:en:Dendrochronology|tree-ring dating]] and [[:en:Radiocarbon dating|radiocarbon dating]]. Furthermore, microfossils such as [[:en:Diatom|diatoms]] and [[:en:Pollen|pollen]] may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Wakō, Saitama|Wako City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|qq=|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}}

This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==

revision.text:
[[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630076]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|'''Overburden'''|覆土|Fukudo}} refers to layers of [[:en:Soil|Soil]] ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|1=stratigraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] in [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]].[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or [[:en:Kiln|kiln ruins]] collapsed. Through [[:en:Archaeological excavation|archaeological excavation]], researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for [[:en:Dendrochronology|tree-ring dating]] and [[:en:Radiocarbon dating|radiocarbon dating]]. Furthermore, microfossils such as [[:en:Diatom|diatoms]] and [[:en:Pollen|pollen]] may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Wakō, Saitama|Wako City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * [[:en:Feature (archaeology)|Archaeological Feature]] * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==

For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or [[:en:Kiln|kiln ruins]] collapsed. Through [[:en:Archaeological excavation|archaeological excavation]], researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for [[:en:Dendrochronology|tree-ring dating]] and [[:en:Radiocarbon dating|radiocarbon dating]]. Furthermore, microfossils such as [[:en:Diatom|diatoms]] and [[:en:Pollen|pollen]] may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Wakō, Saitama|Wako City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630076]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|'''Overburden'''|覆土|Fukudo}} refers to layers of {{ill|Soil|en|Soil|WD=Q36133|lt=Soil}} ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|1=stratigraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature}} in {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology}}.[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology}} and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Kiln|en|Kiln|WD=Q1724341|lt=kiln ruins}} collapsed. Through {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=archaeological excavation}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|Dendrochronology|en|Dendrochronology|WD=Q80205|lt=tree-ring dating}} and {{ill|Radiocarbon dating|en|Radiocarbon dating|WD=Q173412|lt=radiocarbon dating}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|Diatom|en|Diatom|WD=Q162678|lt=diatoms}} and {{ill|Pollen|en|Pollen|WD=Q79932|lt=pollen}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Wakō, Saitama|en|Wakō, Saitama|WD=Q658058|lt=Wako City}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630076]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|'''Overburden'''|覆土|Fukudo}} refers to layers of {{ill|Soil|en|Soil|WD=Q36133|lt=Soil}} ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|1=stratigraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature}} in {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology}}.[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology}} and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Kiln|en|Kiln|WD=Q1724341|lt=kiln ruins}} collapsed. Through {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=archaeological excavation}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|Dendrochronology|en|Dendrochronology|WD=Q80205|lt=tree-ring dating}} and {{ill|Radiocarbon dating|en|Radiocarbon dating|WD=Q173412|lt=radiocarbon dating}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|Diatom|en|Diatom|WD=Q162678|lt=diatoms}} and {{ill|Pollen|en|Pollen|WD=Q79932|lt=pollen}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Wakō, Saitama|en|Wakō, Saitama|WD=Q658058|lt=Wako City}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|Diatom|en|Diatom|WD=Q162678|lt=diatoms}} and {{ill|Pollen|en|Pollen|WD=Q79932|lt=pollen}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{Nihongo|'''Overburden'''|覆土|Fukudo}} refers to layers of {{ill|Soil|en|Soil|WD=Q36133|lt=Soil|1=Soil}} ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|1=Q55524646|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature|1=Feature (archaeology)}} in {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}}.[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Kiln|en|Kiln|WD=Q1724341|lt=kiln ruins|1=Kiln}} collapsed. Through {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=archaeological excavation|1=Archaeological excavation}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|Dendrochronology|en|Dendrochronology|WD=Q80205|lt=tree-ring dating|1=Dendrochronology}} and {{ill|Radiocarbon dating|en|Radiocarbon dating|WD=Q173412|lt=radiocarbon dating|1=Radiocarbon dating}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|Diatom|en|Diatom|WD=Q162678|lt=diatoms|1=Diatom}} and {{ill|Pollen|en|Pollen|WD=Q79932|lt=pollen|1=Pollen}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Wakō, Saitama|en|Wakō, Saitama|WD=Q658058|lt=Wako City|1=Wakō, Saitama}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature|1=Feature (archaeology)}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] {{Nihongo|'''Overburden'''|覆土|Fukudo}} refers to layers of {{ill|Soil|en|Soil|WD=Q36133|lt=Soil|1=Soil}} ({{ill|stratigraphy|ja|土層 (考古学)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stratigraphy|1=Q55524646|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55524646}}) that accumulate within a {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature|1=Feature (archaeology)}} in {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}}.[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305440321000996 The archaeology of overburden: Method within the madness at Švédův Stůl, Czech Republic - ScienceDirect] It is also referred to as {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} (うめど・うめつち・まいど).In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and excavation work, the terms "覆土" (Fukudo) and "埋土" (Umedo) are nearly synonymous when describing accumulated soil within structures. For example, the reference guide "発掘調査のてびき" uses the term "埋土（覆土）" (Umedo/Fukudo) (p. 94). == Overview == Fukudo can contain information about the structure or simply consist of soil that flowed into the area. It provides insights into the characteristics of the structure and its abandonment process. For instance, it may reveal whether the structure's abandonment occurred gradually or abruptly, and whether features like {{ill|subterranean pits|ja|地下式坑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subterranean pits|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Kiln|en|Kiln|WD=Q1724341|lt=kiln ruins|1=Kiln}} collapsed. Through {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=archaeological excavation|1=Archaeological excavation}}, researchers often leave section belts (stratigraphic belts) to observe the soil layers, allowing them to infer the burial and abandonment processes of the structure.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs|2010|pp=93-116}}Such stratigraphic observations are expressed as cross-sectional diagrams known as section drawings. When Fukudo contains unique information about a structure, such as remnants of {{ill|burned wood|ja|焼け落ちた木材|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/burned wood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it can indicate whether the building was destroyed by fire or abandoned. In favorable conditions, researchers can determine materials used in the roof (e.g., soil, thatch), the sequence of destruction, and the types of wood used in its construction. The material may also serve as samples for {{ill|Dendrochronology|en|Dendrochronology|WD=Q80205|lt=tree-ring dating|1=Dendrochronology}} and {{ill|Radiocarbon dating|en|Radiocarbon dating|WD=Q173412|lt=radiocarbon dating|1=Radiocarbon dating}}. Furthermore, microfossils such as {{ill|Diatom|en|Diatom|WD=Q162678|lt=diatoms|1=Diatom}} and {{ill|Pollen|en|Pollen|WD=Q79932|lt=pollen|1=Pollen}} may provide insights into the environment at the time. At the {{ill|Fukiage Shell Mound|ja|吹上貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiage Shell Mound|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Wakō, Saitama|en|Wakō, Saitama|WD=Q658058|lt=Wako City|1=Wakō, Saitama}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, pit dwellings buried under shell layers yielded near-complete pottery. Beneath these layers, undisturbed soil was observed as Fukudo, showing that the site transitioned from a dwelling to a disposal site for pottery and later for shells. This cycle of human activity was identified as the "Fukiage Pattern" by {{ill|Tatsuo Kobayashi|ja|小林達雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuo Kobayashi|1=Tatsuo Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11461911}}, based on observations of Fukudo. == Notes ==
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|ref=harv|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs|authorlink=文化庁|chapter=Chapter IV: Recognizing Soil Layers and Excavating Surface and Inclusion Layers|title=Handbook for Archaeological Excavations - Settlement Site Excavation Edition|pages=93-116|date=2010-05|year=2010|publisher=[[Doseisha]]|isbn=9784886215253}} == See Also == * {{ill|Cutting Relationships|ja|切り合い関係|1=Intercutting relationships|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cutting Relationships|13=User:Immanuelle/Intercutting relationships|qq=}} * {{ill|Feature (archaeology)|en|Feature (archaeology)|WD=Q814254|lt=Archaeological Feature|1=Feature (archaeology)}} * {{ill|Umedo|ja|埋土|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umedo|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|覆土 (考古学)|version=95512445|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:覆土 (考古学)]] [[ja:覆土]] {{wikidata link|Q11630076}} [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Soil]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]
